content:
Hands-On Assignment 1: LLM-Assisted Architecture Design for OPI DPU Operator

Objective: Design a software architecture solution, leveraging a Large Language Model (LLM), to add NVIDIA (or AMD) support to the OPI (Open Programmable Infrastructure) DPU operator.

Background: OPI defines vendor-neutral standards and tooling for DPUs and IPUs. The existing OPI DPU operator currently focuses on Intel and Marvell offload stacks. NVIDIA provides their own standalone DPF (DOCA Platform Framework) operator. The goal is to design an architecture that brings NVIDIA DPU support into the unified OPI operator ecosystem while maximizing the reuse of the existing DPF operator.

Tasks:
1. Engineer prompts that guide the LLM to design an integration pattern (e.g., adapter pattern, sub-operator, or CRD translation layer) between the OPI operator and the NVIDIA DPF operator.
2. Ensure the generated solution strictly aligns with Kubernetes operator patterns and the current OPI architecture.
3. Bonus: Use the LLM to generate a basic foundational Go code skeleton for this integration (e.g., a reconciliation loop adapter).

Before designing, I grounded the architecture in the current state of both projects (via web research of the openshift/dpu-operator repo, Red Hat OpenShift 4.19/4.20 DPU Operator documentation, the Red Hat 'Unifying multivendor DPUs' blog, and NVIDIA/doca-platform docs and system architecture):

OPI DPU operator facts: activated by a singleton DpuOperatorConfig CR (config.openshift.io/v1, mode host/dpu); a vendor-neutral controller-manager plus a dpu-daemon DaemonSet on host and DPU sides; ALL vendor-specific logic is isolated in per-node Vendor Specific Plugin (VSP) pods that implement an OPI vendor-agnostic gRPC contract consumed by dpu-daemon; workloads are requested through a vendor-neutral ServiceFunctionChain CR (networkFunctions: name + container image); inventory/health is exposed as vendor-neutral DPU CRs (oc get dpu). Supported today: Intel IPU E2100, Intel NetSec Accelerator (Senao SX904), Marvell Octeon 10. NVIDIA BlueField-3 is explicitly not supported.

NVIDIA DPF facts: a full standalone platform, not a plugin. DPFOperatorConfig activates it; provisioning controllers (DPU, DPUNode, DPUDevice, BFB, DPUSet, DPUFlavor, DPUCluster) handle BlueField Bundle OS installation via Redfish/DMS with a phase state machine (Initializing / OS Installing / Ready / Error); Kamaji hosts a dedicated secondary Kubernetes control plane whose workers are the DPU Arm cores; service orchestration is Helm/ArgoCD-based via DPUService and the higher-level DPUDeployment; chaining via DPUServiceChain / DPUServiceInterface / DPUServiceIPAM / DPUServiceNAD with ServiceChainSet controllers inside the DPU cluster.

Impedance mismatches identified: (1) imperative gRPC plugin contract vs declarative CRDs; (2) MicroShift-per-DPU vs Kamaji-hosted DPU cluster; (3) SFC container images vs Helm-chart-based DPUService delivery; (4) risk of two operators fighting over the same hardware.

Options analyzed:
Option A - Native NVIDIA VSP reimplementing provisioning directly against DOCA/Redfish: perfect OPI alignment but zero DPF reuse (fails the core constraint; duplicates person-years of BlueField state-machine work).
Option B - Standalone bridge operator translating OPI CRs to DPF CRs as a third top-level component: full DPF reuse but poor Kubernetes-operator hygiene (three writers, ambiguous ownership/finalizer semantics across API groups, unclear upstream home, oc get dpu needs extra glue).
Option C (RECOMMENDED) - DPF-backed VSP: an NVIDIA VSP that satisfies the OPI VSP gRPC contract outward (OPI core completely unchanged, NVIDIA is 'just another vendor plugin' exactly like Intel/Marvell) but is implemented inward as a CRD translation layer that server-side-applies DPF resources; a companion vsp-nvidia-controller manages DPF itself as a sub-operator (Helm install, DPFOperatorConfig ownership, health) and mirrors DPF status into OPI DPU CR conditions. This composes all three suggested patterns: adapter (at the gRPC boundary), CRD translation layer (pkg/translate), and sub-operator (DPF lifecycle management).

Kubernetes operator-pattern alignment enforced in the design: single-writer-per-resource discipline (VSP is sole writer of translated DPF CRs via SSA fieldManager vsp-nvidia; DPF is sole owner of hardware; status flows back through one mirror controller); ownerReferences plus an owned-by label for cross-scope garbage collection with finalizer-driven cascading deletion (SFC delete -> DPUDeployment delete -> DPF teardown); level-triggered idempotent reconciliation where gRPC calls are treated as intent notifications and desired state is re-derived from CRs each pass; explicit condition mapping (DPF phase Ready -> OPI Ready=True; Error -> Ready=False/DPUError; Initializing/OS Installing -> Ready=False/Provisioning; DPF unavailable -> Ready=Unknown/VendorStackUnavailable); translation layer pinned to a DPF API version with golden-file conformance tests, failing closed on unknown versions.

Key mappings: DpuOperatorConfig -> DPFOperatorConfig; labeled nodes -> DPUSet/BFB/DPUFlavor; ServiceFunctionChain networkFunction -> DPUDeployment using a generic parametrized 'nf-wrapper' Helm chart so arbitrary container images fit DPF's Helm delivery model; DPF DPU/DPUService status -> OPI DPU/SFC conditions. Topology decision: accept per-vendor divergence below the API line - for NVIDIA the DPU-side runtime is DPF's Kamaji cluster (no MicroShift, no OPI operator on the DPU side), preserving user-visible uniformity via the status mirror, consistent with OPI's standardize-the-API-not-the-implementation philosophy.

Deliverables produced: architecture_design.md with a Mermaid component diagram and three Mermaid sequence diagrams (activation+provisioning; SFC-to-DPUDeployment workload flow; level-triggered status mirroring and drift repair), a trade-off matrix over 8 criteria, failure-mode analysis, and a 4-phase implementation plan ending in an upstream contrib-VSP submission. Bonus: feature_skeleton.go - a compilable (go build + go vet verified, stdlib-only by design) skeleton containing the VendorPlugin interface mirroring the OPI VSP contract, typed stubs of the DPF resources touched (DPU with phase state machine, DPUSet, DPUDeployment with HelmChartSource), the Translator with ToDPUSet/ToDPUDeployment and the MapDPUPhase condition mapper, a Client seam standing in for controller-runtime server-side apply, and a ReconcileAdapter implementing Init/GetDevices/Create-DeleteNetworkFunction plus a periodic MirrorStatusOnce/Run resync loop, with a compile-time assertion that the adapter satisfies the VendorPlugin contract and fail-closed guarding of the pinned DPF API version.

(Offered follow-up iterations: defend Option C against Option B under challenge; deep-dive the ServiceFunctionChain -> DPUDeployment translation; stress-test failure modes.)

Iteration 2 - design defense and deep dives.

(1) Option C vs Option B, in Kubernetes-native terms. Option B's real advantage is deployment independence, but it loses on four grounds. Ownership: Kubernetes forbids cross-namespace ownerReferences, so a standalone bridge operator translating between two foreign API groups degenerates into label-based pseudo-ownership and hand-rolled garbage collection; in Option C the VSP is lifecycle-managed by the OPI core itself (created/upgraded/removed with DpuOperatorConfig), so no third lifecycle exists. Discovery contract: the OPI controller-manager populates DPU inventory CRs from VSP GetDevices() responses; a bridge would have to write DPU CRs directly, impersonating a vendor plugin and creating a second writer of objects the core believes it owns. Failure semantics: VSP failures propagate through the existing gRPC error path and degrade conditions exactly like Intel/Marvell failures; a bridge fails silently - translation just stops, invisible to the OPI status model. Upstream reality: the VSP seam is the sanctioned extension point with two vendor precedents; a bridge has no natural home repo and doubles the support matrix. Option B would win only if OPI lacked a plugin seam or vendor components were banned from host nodes; neither holds.

(2) Deep dive: SFC -> DPUDeployment translation. Mismatch: SFC networkFunctions[] names bare container images; DPF delivers Helm charts via ArgoCD into the Kamaji DPU cluster. Solution: a single generic 'nf-wrapper' Helm chart with values schema {image, chainName, nfName, interfaces, nvidia.com/bf_sf resource count} rendering a DaemonSet on the DPU nodes with Multus annotations referencing generated DPUServiceNADs. Fan-out per NF: one DPUDeployment + two DPUServiceInterfaces (ingress/egress SFs) + one DPUServiceChain whose order derives from SFC array order (chain position is data, not configuration). Idempotency: deterministic names (<sfc>-<nf> with hash suffix under 63 chars) plus server-side apply make re-reconciles no-ops. Deletion: finalizer dpu.opiproject.org/nvidia-vsp on the SFC -> VSP deletes DPUDeployment -> DPF cascades DPUService/Argo teardown -> VSP confirms absence -> finalizer released. Version-skew insurance: DPUDeployment is DPF's stable high-level abstraction, so most DPF release churn is absorbed by bumping the wrapper chart, not the translator. Honest limit: NFs needing privileged host-side access do not map onto DPF delivery and are surfaced as an Unsupported condition on the SFC rather than half-working.

(3) Stress test. Split-brain with a pre-existing admin-installed DPF: adopt only if DPFOperatorConfig carries an explicit adoption annotation, otherwise raise a conflict condition and do nothing. DPF upgrades: fail closed on unpinned API versions; promote via the golden-file conformance suite as a canary. Scale: per-node VSP is stateless; translation is linear in network functions. Status staleness: bounded by watch-driven mirroring with a 30-second periodic resync floor doubling as drift repair.

Results folded into architecture_design.md as Appendix A.
